kommunikationsstil.beschreibung: Person B writes in one-word sentences, lowercase. "ok", "yeah", "fine". Reads like indifference, but it's precisely measured: enough contact to not leave, too little to land. Nobody writes like that by accident.

horney.interpretation: Person B isn't moving away — Person B is moving slower than asked. That's a difference, and it's a strategy. When the other's frequency goes up, Person B's goes down. When emotional topics land, "sorry, that was a lot" comes out — not as an excuse, but as a stop sign.
evidenz: Reply times double after every emotional opener from Person A
"not today please" — withdrawal named, not explained
No return questions, even when asked directly

berne.interpretation: Person B keeps the tone low. Matter-of-fact, brief, no drama. Feels like maturity — partly is. But when Person A gets emotional, Person B slips into slightly parental mode: "I think you're overthinking this" isn't an adult-to-adult line anymore. That's parent-child, and the roles are clearly set in that moment.
evidenz: "it's fine" — matter-of-factness as deflection, not state
"you're overthinking this" — diagnosis instead of dialogue

bowlby.interpretation: Closeness isn't fought off, it's deactivated. When Person A opens up, Person B goes silent. When Person B opens up — and that does happen, rarely, in half-sentences — the opening isn't built on. Warmth shows up briefly and disappears again before it can become part of a story. Classic avoidant: the capacity is there, the use of it is rationed.
evidenz: "you're cute" — once, no follow-up, never mentioned again
Replies to Person A's monologues: max 1 sentence, often 24h later
No questions about the other person's state

adler.interpretation: Person B's stance: I don't need this more than you do. That's not arrogance — it's calculated. Whoever needs less is less exposed. The math works, but has a cost: Person B can never fully arrive. Not for lack of opportunity, but because arriving would break the system.
evidenz: Never the first to write in the morning
Never asks how the other is doing
Emotional peaks from Person A get defused, not mirrored

goffman.front_stage: The relaxed one. Uncomplicated, no drama, "it's fine". Someone you won't get stress with.
goffman.back_stage_durchbrueche: Three warm messages in four months. "you're cute", "nice to see you yesterday", "i like you too". Each stands alone, never linked, never expanded. The backstage exists — it just doesn't get walked into.
goffman.interpretation: The "easy" performance isn't an accident — it's the product. Person B sells calm, and buys in return the ability to never really be fully present. That's not coldness. That's a very old deal between Person B and themselves.
evidenz: Warmth comes in 10-character bursts, never paragraphs
No references to earlier conversations ever appear

sprachliche_fingerabdruecke.zeichensetzung: No punctuation at the end. Lowercase throughout, even names. No emojis — not even 👍. The absence is the signal.

kern_insight: Person B learned early that "i need you" costs more than "i'm chill" — and hasn't said the first one to anyone since.
